beats.text: An option can be manufactured from the stock and cash by holding a continuously adjusted position.

visual.sub: Replicate the option, find its only fair price

beats.text: Hold delta shares plus a bond, and the portfolio tracks the option's value instant by instant.
If replication costs less than the option, you sell the option and buy the portfolio for riskless profit.
The hedged portfolio carries no exposure to random moves, so it can only earn the riskless rate r.
Two traders who disagree on direction still agree on the price, because beliefs about drift drop out entirely.
Under the risk-neutral measure every asset drifts at r, and pricing reduces to expectation and discounting.

visual.note: Drift becomes r, never the real-world mu

beats.text: Black-Scholes assumes the stock follows geometric Brownian motion, so the log-price is Brownian with drift.

visual.sub: Geometric Brownian motion and four idealizations

beats.text: Proportional returns are normal, which makes the terminal price an exponential of a normal variable.

visual.note: The integrated GBM solution from Ito's lemma

beats.text: The minus one-half sigma-squared term is the Ito correction, the volatility drag on log returns.
Because price is an exponential of a normal, the distribution is lognormal, never negative and right-skewed.
Stacked on GBM are four idealizations: constant vol, constant rate, continuous paths, and frictionless trading.
A quant treats the model as a coordinate system, mapping one arguable number, implied volatility, to a price.
Made continuous, the hedging argument says any derivative value must satisfy a parabolic PDE with no mu.

visual.sub: From the PDE to a closed-form price

beats.text: The PDE is a backward heat equation that propagates the known terminal payoff back to today.

visual.tex: \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} + \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma^2 S^2 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial S^2} + rS\frac{\partial V}{\partial S} - rV = 0
visual.note: Time decay, convexity, drift at r, discounting

beats.text: Solving it with the call payoff boundary condition gives a closed form needing no simulation.

visual.note: European call on a non-dividend stock

beats.text: The two d-terms compare spot to strike, scaled by volatility over the square root of time.

visual.note: N(d_2) is the risk-neutral probability of finishing in the money

beats.text: Put-call parity is a model-free identity: a call minus a put equals a forward on the stock.

visual.note: Any violation is a pure arbitrage

beats.text: At spot one hundred, strike one hundred, twenty vol and one year, the call prices near ten dollars forty.
The price is one number; the Greeks are its partial derivatives, and that is where the money is managed.

visual.sub: Partial derivatives as a risk dashboard

beats.text: Delta is the share-equivalent position; desks buy or sell delta shares to make the book delta-neutral.
Gamma is the curvature, how fast delta itself moves, and it peaks sharply at the money near expiry.
Vega measures P&L if implied vol re-rates and is largest for at-the-money, long-dated options.

visual.note: Gamma and vega are identical for calls and puts

beats.text: Theta is the daily rent a long option pays, the time decay accepted to hold gamma and vega.
A delta-hedged book is long gamma but short theta, a bet that realized vol exceeds the implied vol paid.
Five of six inputs are observable; only sigma is not, so traders run the formula backwards from price.

visual.sub: Inverting the formula to read the market

beats.text: Implied volatility is the sigma that makes the model price equal the quoted market price.

visual.note: Solve numerically by bisection or Newton

beats.text: Because vega is always positive, price is monotonic in sigma, so the inverse exists and is unique.
If the model were true every strike would imply the same sigma; instead the curve smiles or skews.
In equity indices since 1987 the curve is a downward skew, with out-of-the-money puts at higher vol.
The smile is the market pricing crash protection, the constant-vol assumption's error made tradeable.

concepts.title: Where it breaks & what comes next

beats.text: Black-Scholes is the most successful wrong model in finance, and its failures arrive as market-wide losses.

visual.sub: Jumps, stochastic vol, costly hedging, regimes

beats.text: On 19 October 1987 the S&P fell about twenty percent, a twenty-sigma event the lognormal calls impossible.
Continuous paths fail because real prices gap; Merton's jump-diffusion restores the fat left tail.
Volatility is not constant; the Heston model makes variance a mean-reverting random process correlated with the stock.

visual.note: Negative correlation rho produces the equity skew

beats.text: Black-Scholes is the degenerate special case of Heston with vol-of-vol set to zero and constant variance.
Next, Quant 04 turns volatility loose on the yield curve, where the thing being modelled is the rate itself.

visual.sub: Vasicek, Hull-White, CIR and change of numeraire
